description:
You are an operations engineer who has been asked to triage a sudden spike in open incidents.  
    An internal status-page snapshot has already been placed for you at  
        /home/user/internal_status/status.html  

    Your objective: scrape that HTML file in a completely headless, CLI-only manner and prepare a
    triage artefact bundle that other engineers can immediately consume.

    REQUIRED END-STATE
    ------------------
    1. Directory  
          /home/user/incidents  
       must exist (create it if necessary).

    2. Inside that directory create three files whose exact names, order of fields and character
       casing will be checked by an automated test:

       2.1 /home/user/incidents/incident_report.csv  
           • UTF-8 text.  
           • First line is the header (all lower-case):  
                 incident_id,service,severity,state  
           • One subsequent line for every row that appears inside
               the <tbody> of the table whose id is "incidents" in status.html.  
             Preserve the order in which the rows appear in the HTML.  
           Example of the required format (values will match the HTML):  
                 INC001,Auth-Service,High,Open

       2.2 /home/user/incidents/severity_count.json  
           • Minified JSON **without any extra spaces or line-breaks**.  
           • Keys represent severity levels in title-case: Critical, High, Moderate, Low  
           • Values are integers equal to the number of incidents whose **state is NOT "Resolved"**.  
             (States “Open”, “Investigating”, “Monitoring”, etc. are all counted.)  
           • All four keys must be present even when the count is zero.  
           Example of exact formatting (values will be verified):  
                 {"Critical":1,"High":2,"Moderate":0,"Low":0}

       2.3 /home/user/incidents/validation.log  
           • Plain text, one line per incident, in the **exact** sentence format:  
                 Found <ID> <Service> <Severity> <State>  
           • Example line (spacing and casing matter):  
                 Found INC002 Billing-Service Critical Investigating

    3. All three files must be world-readable (chmod 644) so CI pipelines can open them.

    CONSTRAINTS & HINTS
    -------------------
    • Only command-line tooling is allowed; treat this as a “headless browser” data-scrape.  
      curl, cat, grep, sed, awk, perl, python -m http.server, etc. are all fair game—
      but you must not launch anything that requires a GUI.

    • You do **not** need network access; scrape the local file directly.

    • Expect to use roughly 5–10 shell commands: create the directory, extract the table rows,
      normalise the fields, write the CSV, calculate counts for the JSON, and produce the log.

    • When calculating severity_count.json remember to:
      – exclude rows whose state is exactly “Resolved” (case-sensitive),  
      – include all other rows,  
      – include zero-count keys so the JSON always has four entries.

    • Once your files are ready you can verify them yourself with commands such as:  
          cat /home/user/incidents/incident_report.csv  
          cat /home/user/incidents/severity_count.json  
          cat /home/user/incidents/validation.log

    The automated test will open those paths and must find byte-for-byte identical contents to the
    format described above.  No additional files should be created inside /home/user/incidents.
truth:
Internal Status

Current Incidents

IDServiceSeverityState
INC001Auth-ServiceHighOpen
INC002Billing-ServiceCriticalInvestigating
INC003Search-ServiceModerateResolved
INC004Notification-ServiceHighMonitoring
]]>
